nativeRange.habitat: Riparian and marshland environments — typically grows emergent along the margins of streams, ponds, and rice paddies, but tolerates long periods fully submerged

decorPreferences: background planting area with vertical space for tall stems
best displayed in dense groups of multiple stems
nutrient-rich substrate preferred but tolerates plain gravel or sand with root tabs

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large herbivorous cichlids

imageCandidates.notes: Potted nursery photo from Modern Aquarium product page; shows submerged habit with narrow willow-like leaves on tall stems. Likely watermark-free product shot but copyright held by retailer.
Buce Plant product photo (1050x700) showing the submerged form. Retailer copyright.
Secondary Buce Plant product photo (700x700). Retailer copyright.

summary: Hygrophila angustifolia (Willow Hygro, Narrow-Leaf Hygro) is a fast-growing background stem plant from South and Southeast Asia with long, narrow willow-like leaves. An undemanding beginner plant tolerant of a wide range of conditions; aquarium-trade plants are widely treated as a narrow-leaved form of H. corymbosa.
careNotes: Hygrophila angustifolia is a hardy, fast-growing stem plant well suited to beginners and low-tech tanks. Tall stems carry long, narrow leaves giving a willowy appearance; it is best used as a background plant in dense groups of multiple stems, with frequent topping to encourage lateral branching. Modern Aquarium and Buce Plant both list it as easy with moderate-to-high light and fast growth; Buce Plant gives a submerged height of 15-30 cm, but plants grown in the background of larger tanks routinely reach 40-50 cm, so the upper bound here reflects observed aquarium performance rather than the retailer minimum. CO2 is not required — the plant grows well in non-injected setups — but supplemental CO2 noticeably accelerates growth and produces denser, redder-tipped stems under strong light. Substrate is flexible: plain gravel or sand works, though a nutrient-rich aquasoil or regular root tabs plus liquid fertilizer give fuller growth. Tolerates a wide temperature window (~22-28°C), pH 6.0-7.5 (Buce Plant), and the gH/kH ranges shared with H. corymbosa (carried over here because all reviewed retailers treat this plant as a form of corymbosa). Taxonomic note: Flowgrow notes that 'true' Hygrophila angustifolia in the strict botanical sense is probably not in the hobby; the aquarium plant sold under this name is best understood as a narrow-leaved cultivar of Hygrophila corymbosa (often labeled Hygrophila corymbosa var. angustifolia). Minimum tank volume of 75 L is an estimated guideline reflecting the plant's adult height; no source specified an exact figure. Useful for nutrient export — fast-growing stems readily take up ammonium and nitrate, helping suppress algae in well-planted tanks.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings — both Modern Aquarium and Buce Plant describe the standard stem-plant method: trim a healthy top or lateral shoot 10-15 cm long, remove the lowest pair of leaves, and replant directly into the substrate. New roots form quickly. Regular topping and replanting of cuttings maintains a dense, bushy stand and is the standard way of rejuvenating older stems whose bases have lost leaves.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary source. Buce Plant lists Hygrophila angustifolia as an easy beginner stem plant from Southeast Asia, family Acanthaceae, background placement, fast growth, moderate-to-high light, CO2 not required but recommended, pH 6-7.5, submerged height 6-12 in (15-30 cm), propagation by stem cuttings.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 'Hygro Willow' / Hygrophila angustifolia listed as easy with moderate-to-high light, fast growth, midground-to-background placement, CO2 optional, temperature 72-82°F (22-28°C), origin Southeast Asia including India and Bangladesh, propagation by stem cuttings. Provided primary image candidate.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Acanthaceae, Lamiales) and the nomenclatural clarification: 'true Hygrophila angustifolia in the botanical sense does not belong to the group around H. corymbosa and is probably not even in cultivation' — the aquarium plant is a narrow-leaved cultivar of H. corymbosa.
